author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
Consider that the overall 'surface area' of the turbine is based on the surface area of each 'blade' of each face of the turbine.

The calculation of the Betz limit should include those factors when determining how it works.

author: @MrBrelindm
text:
Couldn't you also make a verticle wind generator out of an HVAC squirrel cage blower? I bet it would generate a good deal of torque from a wind coming from any direction too.

I've seen a couple of vertical wind generators on top of a few businesses near Interstate 696 through Southfield and Farmington. Spiral type monolithic blade on them. A little big for an apartment but a good fit for commercial uses. Not nearly as imposing as giant windmills.

Farmington homeowners have opposed cell towers and radio towers so the carriers stealthed one in near the border. It's s cell tower made to look like a towering ponderosa pine tree (which looks oddly out of place with the deciduous canopy below)!

author: @CreativeCircuits
text:
Hi Rob, I was looking at this design you have here and it seems to me that the holes you have opposite the air foils, the holes in the center will redirect the wind that goes into these areas and then with the different angles of which the wind travels through these, seem to act as a brake or governor and never allow the cuboctahedron to reach full potential. It seems to me that the air foils should have a continuous channel to follow to maximize the air flow potential, but I'm no expert...Smile.
The key is to visualize air as a fluid or imagine it as water flowing, if we give it an opening it will go to it and it will act upon the channels of which it flows through.
Ed

author: @kenrowe167
text: Hi Rob. This is undoubtedly the "coolest" looking VAWT I've ever seen, bar none! Are you sure it's utilising aerodynamic lift, though? It's hard to see from the video, but if the blades are pointing outwards at a low pitch angle, then it will only generate lift at very low tip/speed ratios when the resultant wind direction is close to the true wind direction. It will be good to see it generating power, to get an idea of its utility. It doesn't have to be perfect to be useful. You might want to take at look at the Vertogen Ltd. videos on youtube to see a VAWT with variable pitch blades. Not as cool as yours, though.

author: @douglasfuqua7082
text: I believe Dan Mercer posted what I was about to suggest...and that would be to now fill in the triangular areas with slats, as well.  The INTERNALS, with the large holes should be kept, for structural integrity and dimensional strength of the SYSTEM.  I would NOT place cups, etc. anywhere on the horizontal axes of the system, but if placed ABOVE the system, they likely will add additional centrifugal force, sytemically.  Now BELOW the system, a long, vertical set of semi-triangular vanes well should add additional rotational torque + RPMs and efficiency, overall.     ....the reasons for keeping subsystems close to the rotational axis are many.  Centrifugal and centripetal force, for one...we WANT the increased RPM to handle  duties of power generation...(much like an ice skater spinning, at speed...with closely rotational force.  What we DON'T want is much of anything extending far away horizontally from the axis of rotation, DEPRIVING/defeating the RPM we are TRYING to maximize.  Additionally, on a vertical support pole, etc, without guywires or such, that would likely destroy the machine, as a whole...bits n pieces flying outward everywhere, and high winds, with no governing device installed ...well it's likely fatal to the system, and dangerous to passersby and property.   WOW, that was lengthy.  Hope it helps, best wishes on this design, Robert.  ....Crossbow

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that was lengthy - but thanks for all the help and tips mate

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Okay, so the veins are rectifying the wind into a "straight line" wind directed toward the inside at the angle of the veins. To us, the wind is spinning, but relative to each of those vein pannels you just added, the wind is basically traveling in a straight line on the other side.

What if you had one of those wind/vibration devices from earlier sitting behind each of the vein pannels? Maybe with a funnel to further concentrate the wind on the device

Not only would you get the energy from the whole device spinning, but you could also energy harvest from that second layer of wind. 

Maybe another set of vibration devices behind that, not to catch the wind, but if the device was even the slightest off balance, it would cavatate.  Even a slight cavitation would generate additional power.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good ideas thanks for the tips - and - I am most sorry to point this out - but I believe it is vane when it comes to wind and vein when it comes to blood

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text: . Yes, vane not vein.  No worries. It's early over here :)

author: @htmagic
text: RMS, that is a really wild look wind generator. Now put some magnets and a coil to generate electricity!  Would it drag too much from the magnetic field?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @htmagic
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering BTW, were the venetian blinds plastic or metal? Also if you cut fine kerfs in the frame and slid the blind in you'd get more regular and secure mounting. Then tack in place with hot glue or adhesive. And varying the angle of attack on the blades might be worth a look.

text: Seems like something like that will have very little torque and will stall as soon as any load is applied, don't think just spinning in the wind with no load is a good indicator
